title: How Resilient Were Emerging Market Economies Through the 2022-23 U.S. Monetary Tightening Cycle? - Liberty Street Economics
gist: Through the 2022-23 U.S. tightening, more-vulnerable EMEs (LatAm/Russia/India) beat a New Keynesian model's predictions on both financial stress and GDP, while less-vulnerable EMEs (Asia) matched on FX/spreads but underperformed badly on growth — a reversal of the textbook vulnerability ordering that the authors attribute to commodity prices, China, or improved EME policy frameworks.

insights.claim: The 2022-23 Fed tightening was unprecedented over the prior 30 years in both magnitude and speed, with market expectations of the fed funds path shifting up by 4 percentage points between late 2021 and late 2023, the bulk occurring in 2022.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: Sizes the shock that EME assets had to absorb; the concentrated 2022 repricing is the window where spillover stress should have peaked.
insights.quote: market expectations of the fed funds rate path shifted upwards by 4 percentage points between late 2021 and late 2023, with the bulk of the moves occurring in 2022
insights.locator: The Shift to Tighter U.S. Monetary Policy

insights.claim: Monetary tightening that reacts to strong aggregate demand (growth shocks) has less adverse cross-border consequences than tightening reacting to inflation pressures (monetary shocks), because U.S. demand shocks carry positive spillovers that offset higher U.S. rates.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: A reusable lens for trading EME risk on any Fed cycle: distinguish whether hikes are demand- or inflation-driven before sizing EME downside.
insights.quote: monetary tightening has less adverse consequences when reacting to strong aggregate demand (growth shocks) than heightened inflation pressures (monetary shocks)
insights.locator: Model-Inferred Mix of Growth and Monetary Shocks

insights.claim: Blue Chip survey data show U.S. real GDP growth expectations were marked down through almost all of 2022 even as the expected fed funds path was revised up, implying inflation concerns — not growth — dominated Fed policy until growth expectations began rising in December 2022.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: Confirms the 2022 hikes were the more EME-damaging 'monetary shock' variety, raising the bar for the resilience the data ultimately showed.
insights.quote: progressive markdowns to expected growth through almost all of 2022 that occurred while expectations of the fed funds rate (shown earlier) were being revised up
insights.locator: U.S. Real GDP Expectations Were Marked Down over Much of 2022

insights.claim: Foreign-currency-denominated corporate debt is a key EME vulnerability because domestic currency depreciation can impose balance-sheet losses that more than offset the positive net-export effect of a weaker currency.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: Where FX debt is high, EME currency weakness is contractionary, not stimulative — a screen for which EME corporates are most exposed in a strong-dollar regime.
insights.quote: adverse financial consequences from domestic currency depreciation that, in principle, can more than offset the positive effects through net exports of such depreciation
insights.locator: Model-Inferred Mix of Growth and Monetary Shocks

insights.claim: Imperfect anchoring of inflation expectations is a defining EME vulnerability, modeled by firms setting prices off past inflation surprises rather than forward-looking targets, capturing central banks whose inflation targets lack full credibility.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: Backward-looking inflation anchoring is the variable to track when judging whether an EME central bank can ride out an external shock without losing control of prices.
insights.quote: firms rely on past inflation surprises to guide their price-setting decisions rather than being entirely forward-looking
insights.locator: Model-Inferred Mix of Growth and Monetary Shocks

themes.theme: Surprising resilience and the vulnerability reversal

insights.claim: Both EME groups fared better in financial markets than the two-country New Keynesian model predicted, with the more-vulnerable group (LatAm corporate spreads, plus Argentina/Brazil/Chile/Colombia/India/Mexico/Philippines/Russia FX) outperforming by an especially large margin.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: The most fragile EMEs absorbed an unprecedented Fed shock with less financial stress than models imply — a case for being less short EME credit/FX into hawkish Fed cycles than the textbook suggests.
insights.quote: both groups of EMEs fared better in financial markets than the model predicted, with more vulnerable economies outperforming by an especially large margin
insights.locator: What Might Explain These Divergent Outcomes?

insights.claim: More-vulnerable EMEs showed remarkable GDP resilience with output well above the model's growth-monetary shock path, while less-vulnerable Asian EMEs (China, Indonesia, Israel, Malaysia, South Korea, Taiwan, Thailand, Vietnam) saw GDP well below predictions and close to a pure monetary-shock path.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: The conventional vulnerability ranking inverted on growth — the supposedly safer Asian EMEs underperformed, arguing against using static vulnerability indices to position EME equity/growth exposure.
insights.quote: The more vulnerable EMEs (right panel) displayed remarkable resilience, with GDP levels considerably higher than the model-implied path

insights.claim: For less-vulnerable EMEs, exchange rates depreciated largely as the model predicted, but corporate spreads stayed lower than the identified shock mix implied and near a growth-shock-only path — i.e. financial markets behaved better than fundamentals warranted.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: FX did the adjusting while credit spreads stayed compressed — a pattern to watch for whether spread complacency in resilient EMEs is mispricing growth downside.

insights.claim: The authors flag that off-model factors — global commodity prices and China's growth prospects — may have hit more- and less-vulnerable EMEs differently, a channel their New Keynesian model omits.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: Points to commodity exporters (LatAm) benefiting from the post-2022 commodity boom and China-linked Asian exporters being dragged — a trade differentiation thesis the model can't capture.
insights.quote: developments outside of the United States—such as movements in global commodity prices and China’s growth prospects—affected more-vulnerable and less-vulnerable EMEs differently
insights.locator: What Might Explain These Divergent Outcomes?

insights.claim: An alternative explanation is that more-vulnerable EMEs strengthened their monetary and policy frameworks in ways not yet captured by the vulnerability index, implying the index may overstate their fragility.
insights.speaker: Anna Snider
insights.so_what: If structural EME policy improvement is real and underpriced, it argues for a secular re-rating of high-yield EME credit/FX risk premia.
insights.quote: more vulnerable EMEs may have strengthened their monetary and other policy frameworks in ways not yet reflected in the vulnerability index variables
insights.locator: What Might Explain These Divergent Outcomes?
